raw_output:
Corrupt practices under the Representation of the People Act, 1951, are electoral offences that compromise electoral integrity. Section 123 defines these practices: (1) Bribery—offering/accepting anything of value for electoral advantage; (2) Undue influence—using force, threats, or abetment to compel/persuade voters; (3) Personation—voting under false identity; (4) Illegal gratification—inducing voters through freebies or inducements.

Disproportionate asset accumulation by legislators and associates can constitute undue influence under specific conditions:

First, if financial inducements stem from disproportionate wealth, demonstrating quid pro quo for electoral support, it violates Section 123(2). The Supreme Court (Indrajit Gupta case) established that illicit enrichment coupled with electoral conduct constitutes undue influence.

Second, the causative nexus matters. Mere wealth disparity isn't sufficient; evidence must link assets to electoral influence or voter coercion. The Benami Transactions (Prohibition) Amendment Act, 2016, aids investigation of unexplained wealth.

Third, the Anti-Defection Law and the Prevention of Corruption Act complement the RPA 1951. Asset declarations under the Conduct of Election Rules, 1961, provide baseline evidence for corruption detection.

Conclusion: Disproportionate assets alone constitute corrupt practice only when connected to electoral influence or inducement. Establishment requires substantive proof of quid pro quo, not mere suspicion of corruption.
